title: Why your next EA should probably be AI
excerpt: Most EA work is pattern-based. Scheduling, email, meeting prep — it follows rules. Here's the honest case for switching, and where AI still falls short.
author: Simon Insinger
content:
## Most EA work follows patterns

The executive assistant has been one of the most important hires for growing companies for a long time. Calendar, comms, documents, logistics — they keep the machine running.

But here's the thing: most of that work is repeatable. It follows rules. And AI is now good enough to handle the rule-following parts reliably.

## What it actually does

I want to be specific here because "AI assistant" can mean anything from a glorified chatbot to something genuinely useful.

An AI EA we'd build handles:

- Calendar management that understands your priorities and relationships, not just time slots
- Email triage — categorizing, drafting replies, flagging what's urgent
- Meeting prep — pulling relevant context, recent conversations, and background before each meeting
- Travel booking based on your preferences
- Following up on action items across meetings

That's roughly 80% of what a traditional EA does day-to-day.

## The cost math

A full-time EA in a city like SF or NYC runs $60K-90K/year before benefits. Fully loaded, you're at $80K-120K.

An AI EA costs a fraction of that and runs around the clock. No PTO, no turnover risk.

But honestly, cost isn't the strongest argument.

## The real advantage is consistency

An AI EA doesn't forget follow-ups. It processes information faster than any human can. When you send an email at 11 PM, it's already sorted by the time you wake up. When a meeting gets rescheduled, the calendar cascades happen automatically.

It's not smarter than a good human EA. It's just always on.

## Where AI still falls short

Some things still need a person. Navigating tricky relationships, making judgment calls with incomplete information, reading a room — AI isn't there yet.

The smart play is using AI for the systematic 80% and keeping a human (or yourself) for the 20% that requires real judgment.

## Getting started

Most AI EAs can be up and running in under two weeks. The disruption to your existing workflow is minimal.

Whether you do it now or in a year, this shift is happening. Might as well be early.
